analogy: Think of "Later Mural Traditions" through the chapter's own opening images: EVEN after Ajanta, very few sites with paintings have survived which provide valuable evidences to reconstruct the tradition of paintings. It may also be noted that the sculptures too were plastered and painted. The tradition of cave excavations continued further at many places where sculpting... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Later Mural Traditions is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that eVEN after Ajanta, very few sites with paintings have survived which provide valuable evidences to reconstruct the tradition of paintings.

The text explains that it may also be noted that the sculptures too were plastered and painted.

The text explains that the tradition of cave excavations continued further at many places where sculpting and painting were done simultaneously.

The text explains that badami One such site is Badami in the State of Karnataka.

The text explains that badami was the capital of the early Chalukyan dynasty which ruled the region from 543 to 598 CE.

The text explains that with the decline of the Vakataka rule, the Chalukyas established their power in the Deccan.

The text explains that the Chalukya king, Mangalesha, patronised the excavation of the Badami caves.

The text explains that he was the younger son of the Chalukya king, Pulakesi I, and the brother of Kirtivarman I.

The text explains that the inscription in Cave No.4 mentions the date 578–579 CE, describes the beauty of the cave and includes the dedication of the image of Vishnu.

The text explains that thus it may be presumed that the cave was excavated in the same era and the patron records his Vaishnava affiliation.

The text explains that therefore, the cave is popularly known as the Vishnu Cave.

The text explains that only a fragment of the painting has survived on the vaulted roof of the front mandapa.

The text explains that one shows Kirtivarman, the son of Pulakesi I and the elder brother of Mangalesha, seated inside the palace with his wife and feudatories watching a dance scene.

The text explains that towards the corner of the panel are figures of Indra and his retinue.

The text explains that stylistically speaking, the painting represents an LATER MURAL TRADITIONS Queen and attendants, Badami 5

The text explains that aN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 62 extension of the tradition of mural painting from Ajanta to Badami in South India.

The text explains that murals under the Pallava, Pandava and Chola Kings The tradition of painting extended further down south in Tamil Nadu in the preceding centuries with regional variations during the regimes of Pallava, Pandya and Chola dynasties.

The text explains that the Pallava kings who succeeded the Chalukya kings in parts of South India, were also patrons of arts.

The text explains that paintings at the Kanchipuram temple were patronised by the Pallava king, Rajsimha.

The text explains that the artist seems to have derived sources from oral traditions and local versions of the Ramayana and the Mahabharata for painted narration.

The text explains that even today we observe that mural painting on interior and exterior walls of houses in villages or havelis is prevalent in different parts of the country.

The text explains that krishna playing flute, accompanied by Gopikas, Krishna temple, Pundareekapuram (ritual floor painting of Kerala), using vibrant and luminous colours, representing human figures in three-dimensionality.

The text explains that most of the paintings are seen on the walls of shrines and cloister walls of temples and some inside palaces.

The text explains that thematically too, paintings from Kerala stand apart.

The text explains that most of the narrations are based on those episodes from Hindu mythology which were popular in Kerala.

The text explains that more than sixty sites have been found with mural paintings which include three palaces—Dutch palace in Kochi, Krishnapuram palace in Kayamkulam and Padmanabhapuram palace.

The text explains that among the sites where one can see the mature phase of Kerala’s mural painting tradition are Pundareekapuram Krishna temple, Panayanarkavu, Thirukodithanam, Triprayar Sri Rama temple and T rissur Vadakkunathan temple.

The text explains that these paintings are usually made by women either at the time of ceremonies or festivals or as a routine to clean and decorate the walls.

The text explains that some of the traditional forms of murals are pithoro in parts of Rajasthan and Gujarat, Mithila painting in northern Bihar’s Mithila region, warli paintings in Maharashtra, or simply paintings on the walls, be it in a village of Odisha or Bengal, Madhya Pradesh or Chhattisgarh.

Source: NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art, Chapter 5: Later Mural Traditions

revision_bullets: EVEN after Ajanta, very few sites with paintings have survived which provide valuable evidences to reconstruct the tradition of paintings. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
It may also be noted that the sculptures too were plastered and painted. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
The tradition of cave excavations continued further at many places where sculpting and painting were done simultaneously. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
Badami One such site is Badami in the State of Karnataka. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
Badami was the capital of the early Chalukyan dynasty which ruled the region from 543 to 598 CE. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
With the decline of the Vakataka rule, the Chalukyas established their power in the Deccan. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
The Chalukya king, Mangalesha, patronised the excavation of the Badami caves. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
He was the younger son of the Chalukya king, Pulakesi I, and the brother of Kirtivarman I. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
The inscription in Cave No.4 mentions the date 578–579 CE, describes the beauty of the cave and includes the dedication of the image of Vishnu. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)
Thus it may be presumed that the cave was excavated in the same era and the patron records his Vaishnava affiliation. (NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art Ch5 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat LATER MURAL and TRADITIONS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Later Mural Traditions; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Later Mural Traditions through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LATER MURAL and TRADITIONS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Later Mural Traditions, which statement is supported by the source text about AN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 64?
options: The sinuously drawn lines, fluid forms and compact composition exemplify the proficiency and maturity the artists had achieved in the sixth century CE.
The gracefully drawn faces of the king and the queen remind us of the style of modelling in Ajanta.
Their eye- sockets are large, eyes are half-closed, and lips are protruding.
It is noteworthy to observe that the contours of different parts of the face create protruding structures of the face itself.
